sentence_text: A smaller version of the network, Fast YOLO, processes an astounding 155 frames per second while still achieving double the mAP of other real-time detec- tors.
Dog: 0.30 Person: 0.64 Horse: 0.28 Figure 1: The YOLO Detection System.
Dog: 0.30 Person: 0.64 Horse: 0.28 Figure 1: The YOLO Detection System.
Dog: 0.30 Person: 0.64 Horse: 0.28 Figure 1: The YOLO Detection System.
Our system (1) resizes the input image to 448 × 448, (2) runs a single convolutional net- work on the image, and (3) thresholds the resulting detections by the model’s conﬁdence.
Our system (1) resizes the input image to 448 × 448, (2) runs a single convolutional net- work on the image, and (3) thresholds the resulting detections by the model’s conﬁdence.
Our base network runs at 45 frames per second with no batch processing on a Titan X GPU and a fast version runs at more than 150 fps.
Second, YOLO reasons globally about the image when 1 arXiv:1506.02640v5 [cs.CV] 9 May 2016
We pretrain the convolutional layers on the ImageNet classiﬁcation task at half the resolution (224 × 224 input image) and then double the resolution for detection.
Training We pretrain our convolutional layers on the ImageNet 1000-class competition dataset [30].
We train this network for approximately a week and achieve a single crop top-5 accuracy of 88% on the ImageNet 2012 valida- tion set, comparable to the GoogLeNet models in Caffe’s Model Zoo [24].
Detection often requires ﬁne-grained visual infor- mation so we increase the input resolution of the network from 224 × 224 to 448 × 448.
Detection often requires ﬁne-grained visual infor- mation so we increase the input resolution of the network from 224 × 224 to 448 × 448.
We train the network for about 135 epochs on the train- ing and validation data sets from PASCAL VOC 2007 and 2012.
Throughout training we use a batch size of 64, a momentum of 0.9 and a decay of 0.0005.
We continue training with 10−2 for 75 epochs, then 10−3 for 30 epochs, and ﬁnally 10−4 for 30 epochs.
We continue training with 10−2 for 75 epochs, then 10−3 for 30 epochs, and ﬁnally 10−4 for 30 epochs.
For data augmentation we introduce random scaling and translations of up to 20% of the original image size.
While not critical to performance as it is for R-CNN or DPM, non-maximal suppression adds 2- 3% in mAP.
Experiments First we compare YOLO with other real-time detection systems on PASCAL VOC 2007.
We also present VOC 2012 results and compare mAP to current state-of-the-art methods.
We compare YOLO to their GPU imple- mentation of DPM which runs either at 30Hz or 100Hz.
With 52.7% mAP, it is more than twice as accurate as prior work on real-time detection.
YOLO pushes mAP to 63.4% while still maintaining real-time performance.

Fast R-CNN + YOLO is the forth highest scoring method, with a 2.3% boost over Fast R-CNN.
Those ensembles produced small increases in mAP between .3 and .6%, see Table 2 for details.
VOC 2012 Results On the VOC 2012 test set, YOLO scores 57.9% mAP.
On categories like bottle, sheep, and tv/monitor YOLO scores 8-10% lower than R-CNN or Feature Edit.
Fast R-CNN gets a 2.3% improvement from the combination with YOLO, boosting it 5 spots up on the public leaderboard.
R-CNN has high AP on VOC 2007.
YOLO has good performance on VOC 2007 and its AP degrades less than other methods when applied to artwork.
